articles.headline: Right to Disconnect Bill 2025 Introduced in Parliament, Aims for Work-Life Balance
articles.explainer:
• Supriya Sule, MP, introduced a Private Member's Bill in Parliament titled 'Right to Disconnect Bill 2025'.
• The Bill seeks to address the issue of work-life balance in the modern labour market, particularly in the digital age.
• It proposes to grant employees the right to not respond to official calls or communications outside of working hours.
• The Bill aims to protect employees from excessive work demands and promote mental well-being.
• If passed, it could necessitate significant changes in corporate policies and foster a healthier work culture.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: Supriya Sule: Member of Parliament, introduced the bill.
Private Member's Bill: Can be introduced by any MP who is not a minister. Requires one month's notice. Rarely passed.
Right to Disconnect: Concept originating in France, aims to prevent employers from contacting employees outside work hours.
mainsPoints: Discuss the need for a 'Right to Disconnect' in India's evolving digital economy and its potential impact on employee well-being and productivity.
Analyze the challenges in implementing such a law, including its applicability across various sectors (e.g., IT, essential services) and potential resistance from employers.
Evaluate the 'Right to Disconnect' in the context of existing labour laws and constitutional provisions related to the right to livelihood and privacy.
Compare India's approach with international examples (e.g., France, Spain) regarding the right to disconnect and its implications for global competitiveness.
articles.imageDescription: A person looking at a smartphone with a 'No Entry' sign over it, symbolizing the right to disconnect from work.
articles.trivia: France was the first country to introduce a 'Right to Disconnect' law in 2017, allowing employees to ignore work emails outside of office hours.

articles.headline: IndiGo Begins Massive Flight Cancellations Amidst Pilot Rest and Duty Regulations Compliance Issues
articles.explainer:
• IndiGo, India's largest airline, commenced cancelling thousands of flights nationwide, causing significant travel disruption.
• The cancellations stem from difficulties in complying with revised pilot rest and duty regulations mandated by the government (DGCA).
• The new Flight Duty Time Limitations (FDTL) norms aim to enhance safety by ensuring adequate rest for pilots.
• The airline expects operations to stabilize by December 9, indicating a week-long period of severe impact.
• The disruption highlights the operational challenges faced by airlines in adapting to stricter safety regulations and managing crew rosters.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: DGCA: Directorate General of Civil Aviation, India's regulatory body for civil aviation.
FDTL: Flight Duty Time Limitations, regulations governing maximum duty periods and minimum rest periods for flight crew.
Impact: Major disruption to domestic and international air travel, affecting thousands of passengers.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the IndiGo disruption on India's aviation sector, including passenger confidence, airline profitability, and competition.
Discuss the role of regulatory bodies like DGCA in balancing aviation safety with operational feasibility for airlines.
Examine the challenges faced by airlines in implementing revised FDTL norms, considering crew availability, training, and rostering complexities.
Suggest measures to mitigate such disruptions in the future, focusing on better planning, regulatory foresight, and industry-regulator collaboration.
articles.imageDescription: An IndiGo aircraft parked at an airport with 'Cancelled' signs digitally superimposed on the background.
articles.trivia: The DGCA's FDTL norms are based on international standards set by the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO).

articles.headline: Communal Tensions Erupt in Thiruparankundram Over Karthika Deepam Lighting
articles.explainer:
• Communal tensions flared in Thiruparankundram, Tamil Nadu, concerning the lighting of Karthika Deepam.
• The dispute arose over traditional practices and access to certain areas during the festival, involving different community groups.
• Local administration and police intervened to de-escalate the situation and ensure peace.
• Karthika Deepam is an ancient Hindu festival of lights, primarily celebrated in Tamil Nadu, Kerala, and Andhra Pradesh.
• The incident highlights the delicate nature of religious practices and the need for sensitive governance in maintaining social harmony.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India
Role of women and women’s organization, population and associated issues, poverty and developmental issues, urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
Effects of globalization on Indian society
Social empowerment, communalism, regionalism & secularism.
prelimsPoints: Karthika Deepam: Hindu festival of lights, celebrated in Tamil Nadu, Kerala, and Andhra Pradesh.
Thiruparankundram: Famous for its ancient rock-cut cave temple dedicated to Lord Murugan, one of the Arupadaiveedu (six abodes of Murugan).
Context: Communal disharmony often arises from disputes over public spaces, religious processions, or traditional rights.
mainsPoints: Analyze the underlying causes of communal tensions during religious festivals in India and the role of historical grievances and contemporary socio-political factors.
Discuss the challenges faced by local administration and police in maintaining peace and order during such sensitive situations, emphasizing preventive measures and community engagement.
Examine the constitutional provisions related to religious freedom (Articles 25-28) and how they intersect with the state's responsibility to maintain public order.
Suggest strategies for fostering inter-community dialogue and promoting social harmony in diverse regions like Tamil Nadu.
articles.imageDescription: People gathered near a temple with traditional oil lamps lit, indicating Karthika Deepam celebrations.
articles.trivia: Thiruparankundram is one of the six holy abodes of Lord Murugan, known as 'Arupadaiveedu', and is historically significant for its ancient cave temple architecture.

articles.headline: India-ASEAN Summit Concludes, Emphasizing Digital Economy and Green Transition Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-ASEAN Summit concluded with leaders reaffirming commitment to deepen strategic partnership.
• Key focus areas included enhancing cooperation in the digital economy, fostering innovation, and promoting green transition initiatives.
• Discussions covered strengthening supply chains, maritime security, and regional connectivity under India's 'Act East' policy.
• Leaders agreed on joint projects in renewable energy, smart cities, and digital public infrastructure.
• The summit outcomes underscore India's growing engagement with Southeast Asia and shared vision for a sustainable future.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: ASEAN: Association of Southeast Asian Nations (10 member states).
Act East Policy: India's diplomatic initiative to promote economic, strategic, and cultural relations with the Indo-Pacific region.
Key outcomes: Focus on digital economy, green energy, supply chain resilience, maritime domain awareness.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the significance of India's 'Act East' policy in strengthening its strategic and economic ties with ASEAN nations, particularly in the context of emerging geopolitical dynamics.
Discuss the potential for collaboration between India and ASEAN in the digital economy and green transition sectors, outlining specific initiatives and their mutual benefits.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities in enhancing regional connectivity and maritime security cooperation between India and Southeast Asian countries.
Examine how India-ASEAN cooperation contributes to regional stability and prosperity, and its role in countering geopolitical influences in the Indo-Pacific.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and ASEAN member states displayed during a summit meeting.
articles.trivia: ASEAN was founded in 1967 by Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore, and Thailand.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Government's Authority to Regulate Social Media Intermediaries
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court delivered a landmark judgment, affirming the government's power to regulate social media intermediaries.
• The ruling came in response to a batch of petitions challenging certain provisions of the Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021.
• The Court emphasized the need to balance freedom of speech with national security and public order concerns in the digital space.
• It directed the government to finalize a comprehensive regulatory framework, taking into account privacy rights and due process.
• The decision has significant implications for online content moderation, user data protection, and the accountability of digital platforms in India.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Structure, organization and functioning of the Executive and the Judiciary Ministries and Departments of the Government; pressure groups and formal/informal associations and their role in the Polity.
prelimsPoints: IT (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021: Rules framed under the IT Act, 2000, to regulate social media intermediaries and digital news publishers.
Key aspects: Due diligence by intermediaries, grievance redressal mechanism, traceability of messages.
Constitutional Articles: Article 19(1)(a) (Freedom of Speech), Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty including privacy).
mainsPoints: Analyze the Supreme Court's judgment on social media regulation, discussing the delicate balance between freedom of speech, national security, and user privacy in the digital age.
Evaluate the implications of this ruling for social media platforms, digital news publishers, and individual users in India, particularly concerning content moderation and accountability.

Discuss the challenges in drafting a comprehensive regulatory framework for digital platforms, considering issues like algorithmic transparency, cross-border data flows, and technological advancements.
Compare India's approach to social media regulation with global trends and practices, highlighting best practices and potential pitfalls.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel striking a sound block with social media logos in the background, symbolizing legal judgment on digital platforms.
articles.trivia: The Information Technology Act, 2000, is the primary law in India dealing with cybercrime and electronic commerce.

articles.headline: New National Water Policy 2025 Draft Released, Emphasizing Integrated Water Management
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Jal Shakti released the draft of the National Water Policy 2025 for public consultation.
• The policy emphasizes an integrated approach to water resource management, focusing on demand-side management, conservation, and efficient use.
• It proposes measures for climate change adaptation, inter-state water dispute resolution, and community participation in water governance.
• Key features include promoting water-efficient irrigation, recycling treated wastewater, and using non-conventional water sources.
• The draft aims to ensure water security for all, considering growing population, urbanization, and climate challenges.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
prelimsPoints: Ministry of Jal Shakti: Formed in 2019 by merging Ministry of Water Resources, River Development & Ganga Rejuvenation and Ministry of Drinking Water & Sanitation.
Integrated Water Management: Holistic approach considering all aspects of water cycle and uses.
Key proposals: Water pricing, promoting water markets, groundwater recharge, river basin approach.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the salient features of the Draft National Water Policy 2025 and its potential to address India's complex water challenges, including scarcity, pollution, and inter-state disputes.
Discuss the role of climate change in exacerbating water stress and how the new policy proposes to build resilience through adaptation and mitigation strategies.
Examine the importance of community participation and decentralized governance in achieving sustainable water management, citing examples of successful models.
Evaluate the economic and social implications of promoting water-efficient agriculture and industrial practices, and the challenges in transitioning to such models.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting various aspects of water management, including rivers, dams, irrigation, and urban water supply.
articles.trivia: India accounts for about 18% of the world's population but has only 4% of the world's freshwater resources.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Tests Next-Generation Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Technology
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced the successful test flight of its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) technology demonstrator.
• This crucial test involved a controlled landing experiment, simulating the return of a space vehicle from orbit.
• The RLV program aims to significantly reduce the cost of space launches by making rockets reusable, similar to aircraft.
• This achievement marks a major step towards developing India's own two-stage-to-orbit (TSTO) reusable launch system.
• The technology is vital for future space exploration missions, satellite deployment, and potentially space tourism.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: ISRO: Indian Space Research Organisation, India's national space agency.
RLV-TD: Reusable Launch Vehicle - Technology Demonstrator. Experimental vehicle to test various aspects of RLV technology.
Significance: Reduces launch costs, increases launch frequency, promotes sustainable space access.
Other countries with RLV tech: USA (SpaceX, NASA), China.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) technology for India's space program and its potential impact on global space access.
Analyze the economic benefits of developing indigenous RLV capabilities, including cost reduction, increased launch frequency, and fostering a private space sector.
Examine the technological challenges involved in designing and operating RLVs, such as thermal protection, autonomous landing, and structural integrity.
Evaluate India's position in the global space race, considering its advancements in RLV technology and its future aspirations in space exploration and commercial launches.
articles.imageDescription: An artistic rendition of ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) descending for a controlled landing.
articles.trivia: The first successful orbital RLV was the Space Shuttle developed by NASA, which began operations in 1981.

articles.headline: RBI Keeps Repo Rate Unchanged Amidst Persistent Inflation Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) announced its decision to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged at 6.5%.
• This move signals the RBI's cautious stance, prioritizing inflation control despite signs of moderating economic growth.
• The MPC noted persistent inflationary pressures, particularly from food prices and global commodity fluctuations.
• The decision aims to anchor inflation expectations while supporting the economy's resilience.
• Future policy decisions will remain data-dependent, with a focus on achieving the medium-term inflation target of 4%.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Monetary Policy, Fiscal Policy
prelimsPoints: Repo Rate: The rate at which the RBI lends money to commercial banks.
MPC: Six-member committee (3 RBI, 3 govt. nominees) that determines policy rates to achieve inflation target.
Inflation Target: RBI's mandated inflation target is 4% with a +/- 2% band (2-6%).
Stance: 'Withdrawal of accommodation' or 'Neutral' indicate policy direction.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind the RBI's decision to keep the repo rate unchanged, considering the current economic landscape, inflation dynamics, and global headwinds.
Discuss the effectiveness of monetary policy tools, particularly the repo rate, in managing inflation and supporting economic growth in India.
Examine the challenges faced by the RBI in achieving its medium-term inflation target, including supply-side shocks, global commodity price volatility, and fiscal policy coordination.
Evaluate the potential impact of this monetary policy stance on various sectors of the economy, such as banking, industry, and consumer spending.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting the RBI logo with upward and downward arrows, symbolizing policy decisions.
articles.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) was constituted in 2016 and is entrusted with the task of fixing the benchmark policy rate (repo rate).

articles.headline: Government Launches 'Bharat AI Mission' to Catalyze Indigenous AI Development
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government officially launched the 'Bharat AI Mission', a comprehensive initiative to boost indigenous Artificial Intelligence capabilities.
• The mission aims to create a robust AI ecosystem, focusing on research, innovation, skill development, and ethical AI deployment.
• It includes provisions for establishing National AI Centers of Excellence, promoting AI startups, and developing AI-powered solutions for critical sectors like healthcare, agriculture, and governance.
• Funding of ₹5,000 crore has been allocated for the initial phase, with a strong emphasis on public-private partnerships.
• The 'Bharat AI Mission' is seen as a strategic move to position India as a global leader in AI and leverage technology for inclusive growth.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
articles.source: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology
prelimsPoints: Bharat AI Mission: Government initiative for AI development.
Key pillars: Research & Development, Compute Infrastructure, Skill Development, Ethical AI, AI Applications.
Funding: Initial allocation of ₹5,000 crore.
Nodal Ministry: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY).
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and key components of the 'Bharat AI Mission' and its potential to transform various sectors of the Indian economy and society.
Analyze the opportunities and challenges associated with developing indigenous AI capabilities, including data availability, talent retention, and ethical considerations.
Evaluate the role of public-private partnerships and international collaborations in accelerating India's AI journey and ensuring its responsible deployment.
Examine how AI can be leveraged to address India's developmental challenges in areas such as healthcare, education, agriculture, and smart governance.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized graphic showing a brain circuit connected to various sectors like health, agriculture, and education, representing AI's widespread application.
articles.trivia: India is among the top three countries globally in terms of AI skill penetration and talent availability.

articles.headline: New Report Highlights Growing Threat of Cyberattacks on Critical Infrastructure in India
articles.explainer:
• A report by the National Cyber Security Coordinator's office revealed a significant increase in cyberattacks targeting India's critical infrastructure.
• Sectors most affected include energy, finance, telecommunications, and transportation networks.
• The report attributes the rise to sophisticated state-sponsored actors and organized cybercrime groups.
• It calls for enhanced cybersecurity measures, greater public-private collaboration, and investment in cyber resilience.
• The findings underscore the urgent need for a robust national cybersecurity strategy to protect essential services and national security.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
prelimsPoints: Critical Infrastructure: Assets (physical or virtual) essential for the functioning of a society and economy (e.g., power grids, banking systems).
CERT-In: Indian Computer Emergency Response Team, the national nodal agency for responding to cyber security incidents.
Threat actors: State-sponsored groups, organized cybercriminals, hacktivists.
mainsPoints: Analyze the increasing threat of cyberattacks on India's critical infrastructure and its implications for national security, economic stability, and public safety.
Discuss the current cybersecurity framework in India, including the role of CERT-In and other agencies, and identify its strengths and weaknesses in protecting critical assets.
Examine the challenges in combating sophisticated cyber threats, such as attribution, cross-border nature of attacks, and the rapid evolution of technology.
Suggest comprehensive strategies for enhancing India's cyber resilience, including public-private partnerships, international cooperation, skill development, and legal reforms.
articles.imageDescription: A digital padlock icon superimposed on a map of India, with lines connecting to various critical infrastructure symbols.
articles.trivia: The National Critical Information Infrastructure Protection Centre (NCIIPC) is mandated to protect India's Critical Information Infrastructure.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Internal Security
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Basics of Cyber Security, Challenges to Internal Security

articles.headline: India Pledges Increased Contribution to Green Climate Fund (GCF) at COP30 Preparatory Meet
articles.explainer:
• India announced a significant increase in its financial contribution to the Green Climate Fund (GCF) during a preparatory meeting for COP30.
• The enhanced pledge underscores India's commitment to global climate action and its role in facilitating climate finance for developing nations.
• The GCF is a critical financial mechanism under the UNFCCC, supporting climate change mitigation and adaptation projects in developing countries.
• India urged developed countries to fulfill their pre-2020 commitments and scale up climate finance to meet the $100 billion goal.
• This move strengthens India's moral standing in international climate negotiations and emphasizes the principle of Common but Differentiated Responsibilities.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Green Climate Fund (GCF): Financial mechanism of the UNFCCC, established to help developing countries finance climate action.
UNFCCC: United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, international environmental treaty.
COP30: 30th Conference of the Parties to the UNFCCC.
CBDR: Principle acknowledging different capacities and historical responsibilities of countries in addressing climate change.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's increased contribution to the Green Climate Fund (GCF) in the context of global climate finance and its implications for developing countries.
Analyze the principle of Common but Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR) and India's role in advocating for climate justice in international climate negotiations.
Examine the challenges in mobilizing and deploying climate finance effectively, particularly in meeting the $100 billion goal set by developed nations.
Evaluate the broader implications of such pledges for India's foreign policy and its leadership role in climate action among developing nations.
articles.imageDescription: A hand holding a sapling with a globe in the background, symbolizing global climate action and green finance.
articles.trivia: The GCF was established at COP16 in Cancun, Mexico, in 2010.

articles.headline: Union Cabinet Approves New Scheme for Promoting Millets Production and Export
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved a new comprehensive scheme aimed at boosting millets production, processing, and export from India.
• The scheme includes financial incentives for farmers, support for FPOs, and investment in post-harvest infrastructure and value addition.
• It emphasizes promoting millets as 'nutri-cereals' and integrating them into public distribution systems and mid-day meal programs.
• The initiative aligns with the 'International Year of Millets 2023' momentum and India's goal to become a global hub for millets.
• This policy aims to enhance food and nutritional security, support sustainable agriculture, and increase farmers' income.
gsSubTopics: Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
Food processing and related industries in India— scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management.
Public Distribution System— objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security.
prelimsPoints: Millets: Group of small-seeded grasses widely grown as cereal crops; includes Jowar, Bajra, Ragi, Foxtail, Kodo, etc.
International Year of Millets 2023: Declared by UN General Assembly at India's proposal.
Benefits: Drought-resistant, high nutritional value, low carbon footprint.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of millets as 'nutri-cereals' for addressing food and nutritional security challenges in India, especially in the context of climate change.
Analyze the key provisions of the new government scheme for promoting millets production and export, and evaluate its potential impact on farmers' income and agricultural diversification.
Examine the challenges in mainstreaming millets into the Indian diet and agricultural practices, including market linkages, consumer awareness, and processing infrastructure.
Suggest policy measures to further boost the demand and supply of millets, considering their environmental benefits and health advantages.
articles.imageDescription: Various types of millets displayed in bowls, with wheat and rice in the background, highlighting their importance.
articles.trivia: India is the largest producer of millets in the world, accounting for about 40% of global production.

articles.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Discovers Ancient Buddhist Monastery in Odisha
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced the discovery of a large ancient Buddhist monastery complex in Odisha.
• The site, dating back to the 7th-8th century CE, includes stupas, prayer halls, and residential cells for monks.
• Excavations have unearthed numerous artifacts, including Buddhist sculptures, terracotta seals, and pottery.
• The discovery sheds new light on the spread of Buddhism in eastern India and its cultural exchanges with Southeast Asia.
• This finding is expected to boost heritage tourism and further research into Odisha's rich ancient history.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
History of India and Indian National Movement.
prelimsPoints: Archaeological Survey of India (ASI): Premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage in India.
Odisha's Buddhist heritage: Known for sites like Lalitgiri, Udayagiri, Ratnagiri (Diamond Triangle).
Artifacts: Stupas, viharas (monasteries), sculptures (Buddha, Bodhisattvas), seals.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the discovery of an ancient Buddhist monastery in Odisha for understanding the spread and evolution of Buddhism in Eastern India.
Analyze the architectural and artistic features typically found in Buddhist monastic complexes and how they reflect the philosophical tenets of Buddhism.
Examine the role of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) in preserving and promoting India's cultural heritage, highlighting the challenges and opportunities.
Evaluate the potential of such archaeological discoveries to boost heritage tourism and foster cultural exchange, and the need for sustainable development around these sites.
articles.imageDescription: Excavated ruins of an ancient Buddhist monastery with stupas and brick structures, partially restored.
articles.trivia: Odisha, historically known as Kalinga, was a significant center for the propagation of Buddhism, especially after Emperor Ashoka's Kalinga War.

articles.headline: Health Ministry Launches Nationwide Campaign Against Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR)
articles.explainer:
• The Union Health Ministry launched a comprehensive nationwide campaign to combat Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR).
• The campaign focuses on raising public awareness about the judicious use of antibiotics and promoting hygiene practices.
• It involves collaboration with medical professionals, pharmacists, and public health workers to educate communities.
• AMR is a growing global health threat, making infections harder to treat and increasing mortality rates.
• The initiative aligns with India's National Action Plan on AMR and global efforts to contain the 'silent pandemic'.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
articles.source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: AMR: Antimicrobial Resistance, when microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites) evolve to withstand antimicrobials (like antibiotics).
Causes: Overuse/misuse of antibiotics, poor sanitation, lack of new drugs.
One Health Approach: Recognizes the interconnectedness of human, animal, and environmental health in combating AMR.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing threat of Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) as a global public health crisis and its specific implications for India's healthcare system and economy.
Analyze the key components of the nationwide campaign launched by the Health Ministry, evaluating its potential effectiveness in raising awareness and changing public behavior.
Examine the challenges in combating AMR, including irrational use of antibiotics, lack of new drug development, and inadequate infection control practices.
Suggest a multi-sectoral 'One Health' approach to address AMR, incorporating measures in human health, animal husbandry, and environmental sanitation.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting a bacteria resisting an antibiotic pill, symbolizing antimicrobial resistance.
articles.trivia: World Antimicrobial Awareness Week (WAAW) is observed annually from 18-24 November to raise awareness of global AMR.

articles.headline: India's Manufacturing PMI Shows Strong Growth in November, Boosting Economic Outlook
articles.explainer:
• India's Manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) registered robust growth in November, reaching its highest level in 18 months.
• The expansion was driven by strong new orders, increased production, and sustained export demand.
• This positive indicator suggests a healthy manufacturing sector contributing significantly to overall economic recovery.
• Employment also saw a modest increase, indicating improving labor market conditions.
• The strong PMI data reinforces a positive economic outlook for India in the coming quarters.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Industrial policy changes and their effects on industrial growth.
prelimsPoints: PMI: Purchasing Managers' Index, an economic indicator derived from monthly surveys of private sector companies.
Above 50: Indicates expansion; Below 50: Indicates contraction; 50: No change.
Manufacturing Sector: Key component of India's GDP and employment.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the Manufacturing PMI as a leading economic indicator and its implications for India's overall economic health and growth trajectory.
Discuss the factors contributing to the strong performance of India's manufacturing sector in November, including domestic demand, export markets, and government policies.
Examine the challenges that the manufacturing sector continues to face, such as global supply chain disruptions, input cost inflation, and competition.
Suggest policy measures to sustain and further accelerate manufacturing growth, contributing to employment generation and 'Make in India' initiatives.
articles.imageDescription: A bar graph showing an upward trend in a manufacturing index, with factory outlines in the background.
articles.trivia: The PMI is compiled by S&P Global for more than 40 economies worldwide.

articles.headline: Parliament Passes Bill to Amend Forest Conservation Act, 1980
articles.explainer:
• Parliament passed the Forest (Conservation) Amendment Bill, 2025, modifying the Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980.
• The amendments aim to streamline forest land diversions for strategic projects, particularly those related to national security and public utility.
• It seeks to clarify the applicability of the Act to specific types of land, including those recorded as forest but not notified.
• Environmental groups and tribal communities have raised concerns about potential dilution of forest protection and impact on tribal rights.
• The government asserts that the amendments will balance development needs with conservation efforts and simplify regulatory processes.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980: Requires central government approval for diversion of forest land for non-forest purposes.
Key amendments: Exemptions for certain linear projects, land within 100 km of international borders, security infrastructure.
Concerns: Impact on 'deemed forests', tribal communities, and environmental safeguards.
Related Acts: Forest Rights Act, 2006 (FRA).
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the Forest (Conservation) Amendment Bill, 2025, discussing its stated objectives and the concerns raised by environmentalists and tribal communities regarding its potential impact on forest ecosystems and tribal rights.
Examine the historical context and evolution of forest laws in India, highlighting the tension between development imperatives and conservation goals.
Discuss the importance of 'deemed forests' and the implications of the amendments for their protection and management.
Suggest measures to ensure that infrastructure development and national security projects proceed without unduly compromising forest conservation and the rights of forest-dwelling communities.
articles.imageDescription: A dense forest with a blurred image of parliamentary building in the background, symbolizing the intersection of law and environment.
articles.trivia: The Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980, was enacted to check deforestation and conserve forest resources.

articles.headline: India-Bangladesh Joint River Commission Meeting Addresses Teesta Water Sharing and Flood Management
articles.explainer:
• The India-Bangladesh Joint River Commission (JRC) held its latest meeting, focusing on crucial bilateral water-sharing issues.
• Discussions primarily revolved around the long-pending Teesta River water-sharing agreement and flood management strategies.
• Both nations reiterated their commitment to resolve transboundary river issues through dialogue and cooperation.
• Other agenda items included data sharing, riverine pollution control, and joint basin management projects.
• The meeting aims to strengthen water resource cooperation, vital for the livelihoods of millions in both countries.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Joint River Commission (JRC): Established in 1972, facilitates cooperation between India and Bangladesh on common rivers.
Teesta River: A major transboundary river flowing through Sikkim, West Bengal (India), and Bangladesh.
Other common rivers: Ganges, Brahmaputra, Meghna (collectively known as GBM basin).
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the Joint River Commission meetings in fostering cooperation between India and Bangladesh on transboundary river management.
Discuss the complexities surrounding the Teesta River water-sharing dispute and its implications for bilateral relations and regional development.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in joint flood management strategies between India and Bangladesh, considering climate change impacts and infrastructure development.

articles.headline: New Education Policy Implements Advanced Digital Learning Framework for Higher Education

articles.headline: Government Considers Introduction of New GST Slab for Luxury and Sin Goods
articles.explainer:
• The GST Council is reportedly considering the introduction of a new, higher GST slab specifically for luxury and sin goods.
• This move aims to simplify the existing multi-tiered GST structure and potentially increase revenue collection.
• The proposal suggests a slab above the current highest rate of 28%, targeting items like high-end automobiles, tobacco products, and aerated drinks.
• The decision, if approved, would require amendments to the GST Act and could face resistance from certain industries.
• The rationalization of GST slabs is a continuous process aimed at improving tax efficiency and compliance.
gsSubTopics: Government Budgeting.
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
prelimsPoints: GST: Goods and Services Tax, an indirect tax applicable throughout India.
GST Council: Constitutional body chaired by the Union Finance Minister, makes recommendations on GST issues.
Current slabs: 0%, 5%, 12%, 18%, 28% (plus cess on certain items).
Sin Goods: Goods considered harmful, e.g., tobacco, alcohol (though alcohol is outside GST).
mainsPoints: Discuss the rationale behind introducing a new, higher GST slab for luxury and sin goods, and its potential impact on revenue generation and consumption patterns.
Analyze the implications of GST slab rationalization for various industries, consumers, and the overall tax structure in India.
Examine the functioning of the GST Council in making key decisions related to the indirect tax regime and the challenges of achieving consensus among states.
Evaluate the effectiveness of GST in achieving its objectives of simplifying taxation, boosting economic growth, and enhancing tax compliance since its implementation.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the 'Right to Disconnect Bill 2025', consider the following statements:
1. It is a Private Member's Bill introduced by a Member of Parliament.
2. Its primary objective is to regulate the use of Artificial Intelligence in the workplace.
3. The concept of 'Right to Disconnect' originated in India and is unique to its labour laws.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The bill was introduced by Supriya Sule as a Private Member's Bill. Statement 2 is incorrect: Its primary objective is to bring work-life balance by allowing employees to disconnect from work outside working hours, not to regulate AI use. Statement 3 is incorrect: The concept of 'Right to Disconnect' originated in countries like France.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following bodies is responsible for issuing Flight Duty Time Limitations (FDTL) norms for airlines in India?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Directorate General of Civil Aviation (DGCA) is the regulatory body for civil aviation in India and is responsible for issuing FDTL norms to ensure aviation safety and regulate flight crew duty periods and rest requirements.

prelimsMocks.question:
Regarding the Green Climate Fund (GCF), which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. It is a financial mechanism under the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC).
2. Its primary goal is to provide financial assistance only to developed countries for climate change mitigation.
3. India has recently pledged an increased contribution to the GCF.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The GCF is indeed a financial mechanism of the UNFCCC. Statement 2 is incorrect: The GCF's primary goal is to support climate change mitigation and adaptation projects in developing countries, not developed countries. Statement 3 is correct: India recently pledged an increased contribution to the GCF.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the 'Bharat AI Mission':
1. It is an initiative launched by the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology.
2. Its objective is to promote indigenous Artificial Intelligence capabilities in India.
3. It focuses solely on AI applications in the defence sector.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct: The 'Bharat AI Mission' is launched by MeitY to boost indigenous AI. Statement 3 is incorrect: While defence is a focus area, the mission aims to promote AI across critical sectors like healthcare, agriculture, and governance, not solely defence.

prelimsMocks.question:
The term 'Nutri-Cereals' is often associated with which of the following crops, promoted for their nutritional benefits and climate resilience?
(a) Wheat and Rice
(b) Maize and Barley
(c) Millets (e.g., Jowar, Bajra, Ragi)
(d) Sugarcane and Cotton
prelimsMocks.explanation: Millets, such as Jowar, Bajra, and Ragi, are collectively referred to as 'Nutri-Cereals' due to their high nutritional value (rich in protein, fiber, vitamins, and minerals) and their ability to grow in arid conditions, making them climate-resilient crops.

mainsMocks.question: The introduction of the 'Right to Disconnect Bill 2025' signifies a crucial step towards redefining work-life boundaries in India's digital economy. mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the Bill's purpose. Discuss positive implications (mental health, productivity, equity). Discuss challenges (industry specificities, enforcement, impact on global competitiveness, gig economy). Conclude with balancing employee welfare and economic realities.

Analyze the role of regulatory bodies in ensuring both safety and operational stability, and suggest measures to prevent such widespread service disruptions. (250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure: Airports; Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors; GS2: Public/Civil service values and Ethics in Public Administration.

Suggest solutions: proactive planning, industry-regulator dialogue, infrastructure investment, technological solutions, robust crew management systems, and transparent communication.

mainsMocks.question: Communal tensions arising from religious practices, as witnessed in Thiruparankundram, underscore the fragility of social harmony in diverse societies. Analyze contributing factors: historical grievances, political opportunism, misinformation, lack of trust, socio-economic disparities, and inadequate local governance. GS1.headline: Communal Tensions Erupt in Thiruparankundram Over Karthika Deepam Lighting
GS1.explainer:
• Communal tensions flared in Thiruparankundram, Tamil Nadu, concerning the lighting of Karthika Deepam.
• The dispute arose over traditional practices and access to certain areas during the festival, involving different community groups.
• Local administration and police intervened to de-escalate the situation and ensure peace.
• Karthika Deepam is an ancient Hindu festival of lights, primarily celebrated in Tamil Nadu, Kerala, and Andhra Pradesh.
• The incident highlights the delicate nature of religious practices and the need for sensitive governance in maintaining social harmony.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India
Role of women and women’s organization, population and associated issues, poverty and developmental issues, urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
Effects of globalization on Indian society
Social empowerment, communalism, regionalism & secularism.
prelimsPoints: Karthika Deepam: Hindu festival of lights, celebrated in Tamil Nadu, Kerala, and Andhra Pradesh.
Thiruparankundram: Famous for its ancient rock-cut cave temple dedicated to Lord Murugan, one of the Arupadaiveedu (six abodes of Murugan).
Context: Communal disharmony often arises from disputes over public spaces, religious processions, or traditional rights.
mainsPoints: Analyze the underlying causes of communal tensions during religious festivals in India and the role of historical grievances and contemporary socio-political factors.
Discuss the challenges faced by local administration and police in maintaining peace and order during such sensitive situations, emphasizing preventive measures and community engagement.
Examine the constitutional provisions related to religious freedom (Articles 25-28) and how they intersect with the state's responsibility to maintain public order.
Suggest strategies for fostering inter-community dialogue and promoting social harmony in diverse regions like Tamil Nadu.
GS1.imageDescription: People gathered near a temple with traditional oil lamps lit, indicating Karthika Deepam celebrations.
GS1.trivia: Thiruparankundram is one of the six holy abodes of Lord Murugan, known as 'Arupadaiveedu', and is historically significant for its ancient cave temple architecture.

GS1.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Discovers Ancient Buddhist Monastery in Odisha
GS1.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced the discovery of a large ancient Buddhist monastery complex in Odisha.
• The site, dating back to the 7th-8th century CE, includes stupas, prayer halls, and residential cells for monks.
• Excavations have unearthed numerous artifacts, including Buddhist sculptures, terracotta seals, and pottery.
• The discovery sheds new light on the spread of Buddhism in eastern India and its cultural exchanges with Southeast Asia.
• This finding is expected to boost heritage tourism and further research into Odisha's rich ancient history.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
History of India and Indian National Movement.
prelimsPoints: Archaeological Survey of India (ASI): Premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage in India.
Odisha's Buddhist heritage: Known for sites like Lalitgiri, Udayagiri, Ratnagiri (Diamond Triangle).
Artifacts: Stupas, viharas (monasteries), sculptures (Buddha, Bodhisattvas), seals.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the discovery of an ancient Buddhist monastery in Odisha for understanding the spread and evolution of Buddhism in Eastern India.
Analyze the architectural and artistic features typically found in Buddhist monastic complexes and how they reflect the philosophical tenets of Buddhism.
Examine the role of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) in preserving and promoting India's cultural heritage, highlighting the challenges and opportunities.
Evaluate the potential of such archaeological discoveries to boost heritage tourism and foster cultural exchange, and the need for sustainable development around these sites.
GS1.imageDescription: Excavated ruins of an ancient Buddhist monastery with stupas and brick structures, partially restored.
GS1.trivia: Odisha, historically known as Kalinga, was a significant center for the propagation of Buddhism, especially after Emperor Ashoka's Kalinga War.

GS2.headline: Right to Disconnect Bill 2025 Introduced in Parliament, Aims for Work-Life Balance
GS2.explainer:
• Supriya Sule, MP, introduced a Private Member's Bill in Parliament titled 'Right to Disconnect Bill 2025'.
• The Bill seeks to address the issue of work-life balance in the modern labour market, particularly in the digital age.
• It proposes to grant employees the right to not respond to official calls or communications outside of working hours.
• The Bill aims to protect employees from excessive work demands and promote mental well-being.
• If passed, it could necessitate significant changes in corporate policies and foster a healthier work culture.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: Supriya Sule: Member of Parliament, introduced the bill.
Private Member's Bill: Can be introduced by any MP who is not a minister. Requires one month's notice. Rarely passed.
Right to Disconnect: Concept originating in France, aims to prevent employers from contacting employees outside work hours.
mainsPoints: Discuss the need for a 'Right to Disconnect' in India's evolving digital economy and its potential impact on employee well-being and productivity.
Analyze the challenges in implementing such a law, including its applicability across various sectors (e.g., IT, essential services) and potential resistance from employers.
Evaluate the 'Right to Disconnect' in the context of existing labour laws and constitutional provisions related to the right to livelihood and privacy.
Compare India's approach with international examples (e.g., France, Spain) regarding the right to disconnect and its implications for global competitiveness.
GS2.imageDescription: A person looking at a smartphone with a 'No Entry' sign over it, symbolizing the right to disconnect from work.
GS2.trivia: France was the first country to introduce a 'Right to Disconnect' law in 2017, allowing employees to ignore work emails outside of office hours.

GS2.headline: India-ASEAN Summit Concludes, Emphasizing Digital Economy and Green Transition Cooperation
GS2.explainer:

• Key focus areas included enhancing cooperation in the digital economy, fostering innovation, and promoting green transition initiatives.

• Leaders agreed on joint projects in renewable energy, smart cities, and digital public infrastructure.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Government's Authority to Regulate Social Media Intermediaries
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court delivered a landmark judgment, affirming the government's power to regulate social media intermediaries.
• The ruling came in response to a batch of petitions challenging certain provisions of the Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021.
• The Court emphasized the need to balance freedom of speech with national security and public order concerns in the digital space.
• It directed the government to finalize a comprehensive regulatory framework, taking into account privacy rights and due process.
• The decision has significant implications for online content moderation, user data protection, and the accountability of digital platforms in India.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.

prelimsPoints: IT (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021: Rules framed under the IT Act, 2000, to regulate social media intermediaries and digital news publishers.
Key aspects: Due diligence by intermediaries, grievance redressal mechanism, traceability of messages.
Constitutional Articles: Article 19(1)(a) (Freedom of Speech), Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty including privacy).
mainsPoints: Analyze the Supreme Court's judgment on social media regulation, discussing the delicate balance between freedom of speech, national security, and user privacy in the digital age.

Discuss the challenges in drafting a comprehensive regulatory framework for digital platforms, considering issues like algorithmic transparency, cross-border data flows, and technological advancements.

GS2.imageDescription: A gavel striking a sound block with social media logos in the background, symbolizing legal judgment on digital platforms.
GS2.trivia: The Information Technology Act, 2000, is the primary law in India dealing with cybercrime and electronic commerce.

• Both nations reiterated their commitment to resolve transboundary river issues through dialogue and cooperation.
• Other agenda items included data sharing, riverine pollution control, and joint basin management projects.
• The meeting aims to strengthen water resource cooperation, vital for the livelihoods of millions in both countries.

GS3.headline: IndiGo Begins Massive Flight Cancellations Amidst Pilot Rest and Duty Regulations Compliance Issues
GS3.explainer:
• IndiGo, India's largest airline, commenced cancelling thousands of flights nationwide, causing significant travel disruption.
• The cancellations stem from difficulties in complying with revised pilot rest and duty regulations mandated by the government (DGCA).
• The new Flight Duty Time Limitations (FDTL) norms aim to enhance safety by ensuring adequate rest for pilots.
• The airline expects operations to stabilize by December 9, indicating a week-long period of severe impact.
• The disruption highlights the operational challenges faced by airlines in adapting to stricter safety regulations and managing crew rosters.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: DGCA: Directorate General of Civil Aviation, India's regulatory body for civil aviation.
FDTL: Flight Duty Time Limitations, regulations governing maximum duty periods and minimum rest periods for flight crew.
Impact: Major disruption to domestic and international air travel, affecting thousands of passengers.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the IndiGo disruption on India's aviation sector, including passenger confidence, airline profitability, and competition.
Discuss the role of regulatory bodies like DGCA in balancing aviation safety with operational feasibility for airlines.
Examine the challenges faced by airlines in implementing revised FDTL norms, considering crew availability, training, and rostering complexities.
Suggest measures to mitigate such disruptions in the future, focusing on better planning, regulatory foresight, and industry-regulator collaboration.
GS3.imageDescription: An IndiGo aircraft parked at an airport with 'Cancelled' signs digitally superimposed on the background.
GS3.trivia: The DGCA's FDTL norms are based on international standards set by the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO).

GS3.headline: New National Water Policy 2025 Draft Released, Emphasizing Integrated Water Management
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Jal Shakti released the draft of the National Water Policy 2025 for public consultation.
• The policy emphasizes an integrated approach to water resource management, focusing on demand-side management, conservation, and efficient use.
• It proposes measures for climate change adaptation, inter-state water dispute resolution, and community participation in water governance.
• Key features include promoting water-efficient irrigation, recycling treated wastewater, and using non-conventional water sources.
• The draft aims to ensure water security for all, considering growing population, urbanization, and climate challenges.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.

Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
prelimsPoints: Ministry of Jal Shakti: Formed in 2019 by merging Ministry of Water Resources, River Development & Ganga Rejuvenation and Ministry of Drinking Water & Sanitation.
Integrated Water Management: Holistic approach considering all aspects of water cycle and uses.
Key proposals: Water pricing, promoting water markets, groundwater recharge, river basin approach.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the salient features of the Draft National Water Policy 2025 and its potential to address India's complex water challenges, including scarcity, pollution, and inter-state disputes.
Discuss the role of climate change in exacerbating water stress and how the new policy proposes to build resilience through adaptation and mitigation strategies.
Examine the importance of community participation and decentralized governance in achieving sustainable water management, citing examples of successful models.
Evaluate the economic and social implications of promoting water-efficient agriculture and industrial practices, and the challenges in transitioning to such models.
